sentences.text: The survivor is Hanns Saenger.
The interviewer Heather Rossevitch.
In Johannesburg, South Africa.
English language.
Not to the full extent of what we found out afterwards.
But yes, we were aware that things were happening.
And people disappeared.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And one couldn't trace them-- one didn't know where they were.
No, they just disappeared.
And in the same way as I was taken away from school.
Well, they lost their jobs if they were working.
Yes.
Or they lost their business, because it was smashed or it was looted.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was a general atmosphere of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the great distraction for Hitler was anti-Semitism.
I mean, he covered up what was really wrong in Germany, because he used the anti-Semitic persecution to distract the population.
It should have done more than it did.
They couldn't believe that it was happening.
And my late father used to coin a phrase that the German Jews are stuck to their furniture.
They-- they were reluctant to lose their possessions.
And they couldn't believe that a civilized country like Germany should change like that.
Oh, clearly, clearly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He saw it very, very clearly.
Not at all.
But he was brought up in Poland.
And he lived through pogroms.
And so did his father.
And he knew, when he was a boy, that a Jew could not walk on the pavement if a Polish officer came the other way.
He had to get off the pavement.
And that's why I say, I think he had a sensitivity, which-- which the German Jews didn't have.
Oh, long before.
Well, in '33-- I was bar mitzvah in '32.
So I was just over 13.
To me?
On the 15th of April, 1933, we went to school as usual.
And the SS came into the classroom and asked whether there were any Jews whose fathers had not served in World War I-- would they stand up.
So I stood up, because my father only had one eye and wasn't taken for the army in World War I.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The few-- and I don't know how many there were, but there were very few in the class-- were asked to leave.
And as we came out of the classroom, the Nazis picked us up and put us onto trucks-- that already had many Jewish kids of our age on the truck.
Yes.
There were still other Jews.
This was only Jews whose father had not served in World War I.
Well, that suited them that morning, I suppose.
And they drove us to a railway siding outside Berlin, and loaded us onto cattle trucks.
And they took us away.
Now, you couldn't see where you were going.
You could only see a strip of light on the top of the truck.
And I'm really not certain where we went, except that I have puzzled on the maps.
We went into some swamp land.
And I believe that, according to the maps that I've looked at, that we must have been in the area of Landsberg, which was on the Spree.
The Spree is the river that went through Berlin, and went into very swampy territory.
And there was a school camp, made up of tents, where we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] camped.
We could just stand.
I mean, there was no room at all.
We were packed in like sardines.
About two and a half, three hours.
Filthy.
Nothing.
And when we got out there, this whole camp was manned by Hitler Youth.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were about 16 or 17 years old, and they were all in Nazi uniforms.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the rules were that we got bread and water twice a day.
And for exercise, morning and afternoon, we-- we had to run in a circle, like-- like circus horses are trained.
And the Nazis used to stand in the center with those long whips, except that they hit you properly, not-- didn't touch you, but hit you, so that you would run faster.
We were not allowed to talk to each other.
A couple hours.
Yeah.
And kids used to faint.
We weren't used to this sort of exercise.
Now, every morning, they pulled a name out of the-- out of a box.
And that boy was put into the middle of the swamp, and we had to stand in a half circle and see him sink.
And that took over two hours.
And that's a scene that has been with me all my life-- that every day, one boy of my age was killed, was drowned, until he disappeared.
I think that it was less frightening to see what was happening than the thought as to whose name would be drawn for tomorrow, because it was quite obvious it might be your name.
And this went on for over two months.
One boy a day.

And at the end of June, I think it was the 21st of June, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] without saying anything, they loaded us into trucks again, those that were left, and put us back into cattle trucks, and took us back to a siding outside Berlin and unloaded us.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know that I walked over four hours.
No.
You could-- you weren't allowed to talk to each other.
No.
First, we had to sleep on the floor, on the ground.
Subsequently, they brought in sort of a type of stretcher, wooden stretchers.
And they didn't provide blankets.
Well, in Germany, April, May is spring.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And obviously, we had no blankets.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there were no facilities at all.
There was no purpose to the whole exercise except to-- to kill a few Jews, and to-- and to-- mistreat you because you were a Jew.
Well, they whipped you, and they punched you.
And the discipline was very strict.
I imagine that we must have been [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about 150, 180 boys.
Yes, yes.
No, these-- they all came from Berlin.
It was tents.
And there was one sort of central type of barrack, as a-- as a dining area.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there were no facilities.
I don't know.
I never saw it.
All German.
As far as I know, yes.
No.
So the whole day, you exercised.
Well, we spent two-odd hours seeing a boy drown.
And we were exercised two hours, in the morning and two hours in the afternoon.
So that was our day.
And as soon as it got dark, we went to sleep.
I don't remember exactly.
No.
No.
Yes, we were given a pair of-- one pair of-- a pair of khaki trousers and a-- and a khaki shirt.
That-- that-- we had to wear that same outfit every day for two months.
No.
You develop a hopelessness because you realize that your family doesn't know where you are.
And there was no communication.
I don't even remember that they ever took our names.
But I may well be wrong-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because they must have had the names in order to put them in the box.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I know that the teacher of the class tried very hard to help my father to find me.
They weren't able to trace me.
They just could not find a trace of where we were.
And in fact, my father started saying Kaddish for me.
No.
Well, they-- they rounded us up early in the morning.
Put us into-- they didn't say anything but they put us into a truck, drove us to a railway siding, put us back into cattle trucks, and dropped us outside Berlin in a-- on a siding, a railway siding.
And we started walking.
I had no idea where I was.
But after about three or four hours, I started seeing landmarks that-- I managed to find my way home.
I had no money on me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I certainly couldn't ask anyone where to go, because as a Jew, I could only expect to be mistreated by whoever I would speak to.
And we must have looked very neglected anyway, after all that experience.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] after about four hours, I got home.
I found my way home.
I think it was the 21st of June, 1933.
Well, my mother fainted when she saw me.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as soon as she came around, she phoned for my dad.
And he came home from the office.
And there was a very emotional reunion, because as I said, he was a very religious man.
And he said, this is a miracle that he hadn't ever dreamt of.
And he was not going to sleep another night in Germany.
We were going to do leave that day.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he went to the station, and he booked us tickets for us to leave in the afternoon-- in the afternoon for Paris.
Yeah.
That's when we left.
An empty shoe box was all that my father allowed.
He said that everybody must take an empty shoe box and a piece of string.
And I said, what on earth do we want an empty shoe box for?
He says, you'll see.
Of course, the reason for the empty shoe box became clear during the journey, because after we crossed the French border, there was a knock on the compartment.
And the-- the French consul stood there, with his diplomatic bag, in which he had my father's money.
And he set himself down.
And he started counting 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 3, 4, and he kept his quarter and gave us three quarters.
And we needed the shoe boxes to put the money into.
And we arrived in Paris with five shoe boxes full of money.
Well, that's the sort of man he was.
He organized it.
Yes, we were each given a little, like a school bag, a little suitcase.
And he said, anything you can get in there, you can take with you.
But of course, you couldn't get anything into it.
And I think I took some miniature motor cars with me-- I don't remember.
Nothing.
Nothing.
We left our apartment standing as it is.
Well, it was obviously-- well, most of the contents must have been stolen, because, I mean, we never went back.

But on the-- on the day of the funeral of my mother, a German [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] official arrived with a written guarantee giving my father a guaranteed return, to leave Germany again, if he would come in and open the safe in which he said his money was.
And my father said, well, you can see we've just come back from a funeral.
I'll come back when things sort out-- settle down.
And this man was genuinely embarrassed, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] because he realized that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother had died.
And in France, when there's a death in the-- in the home, the building is draped in black.
They don't have [NON-ENGLISH], they have undertakers.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And my father left him on the basis, when I'm ready to come back.
I'll come back.
But he had no intention of ever doing it.
In any case, there was no money in the safe.
The safe was empty.
My father had very good friends-- they were Polish Jews-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] who had the same sort of business as he had in Germany.
They'd been friends for many, many years.
In fact, they used to go on holiday every year together.
And he had arranged for them that [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] he would signal them somehow when we were coming out.
And they had found us a furnished flat, or a furnished apartment.
So when we arrived, we had a home to go to.
They helped to enroll my brother and I into a boarding school [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] where we went to school.
We couldn't speak French at the time.
So the first thing was-- that's why my father wanted us to be in a boarding school, so that we would learn the language quickly.
Also, he couldn't cope with my mother, who was very ill, and our education at the same time.
So we lived at school.
At the same school?
Yes, there was a primary school, and a-- my brother was four years younger than I.
And he found it very difficult.
He, in fact, refused to-- to-- he said, he doesn't know why we left Germany, and he wants to go back home where people can understand him.
He suffered very badly.
Well, I was older.
I had been through more hardships.
So that for me, it was a relief to be out of Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we went to school.
Well, I wrote-- this was 1933 that we arrived.
And I wrote my matric 1934, '35.
In France, you write two matrics-- one, the first matric is the school-leaving matric.
And the second one is one year later, if you go to university, you've got to write a second exam.
And I had no intention of going to university.
So I left with the first baccalaureate.
And it's then that I found out that I could not get a work permit as an alien, because I would have to serve three years in the French army.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And my father said, no ways can you serve in the French army.
We have no valid passports.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We left Germany, and it's quite obvious that there's going to be a war between Germany and France, sooner or later.
And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we didn't know where to go.
So he went to the chief rabbi of France, who, incidentally, was Rabbi Kaplan.
He died last year.
And he spent many years in a German concentration camp, after the French occupation.
And he asked the chief rabbi, where can we go?
And the rabbi said, well, he'll think about it.
And a few days later, he found us.
And he said, I've just realized I've got family in South Africa.
Of course, my father had never heard of South Africa.
So I looked up the atlas, and I found Cape Town and Johannesburg.
And I showed it to my dad.
And he said, do people live there?
I said, well, they must do.
There-- there are towns there, so there must be some people living.
Anyway, we wrote to the-- no, we didn't write.
They wrote to us.
The relations of the rabbi wrote to us, and said that they came-- they would be coming to London shortly.
And why don't we come over to London and we can ask all the questions we'd like to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and they'd be happy to help us.
And we met them subsequently.
And I think my father conversed with them in Yiddish, because he couldn't speak-- he couldn't speak English.
Nor could I.
And they were some very helpful, very warm, well-known South African Jewish family.
And the long and the short is, as a result of that connection, we-- we decided to come to South Africa.
